title: Second quantization of anyons and spin-anyon duality
authors: Priyanshi Bhasin, Diptiman Sen, Tanmoy Das
abstract: Anyons exhibit a non-trivial interplay between local exclusion rules and non-local braiding and exchange phases, making a consistent commutation algebra and second-quantized formulation challenging. We develop an algebraic framework for Abelian anyons in one dimension with statistical phase $θ$ = $π$/N that enforces a finite on-site occupancy of N-1 anyons with the exchange phase $θ$ between different sites. Moreover, we introduce an exact Jordan-Wigner duality between $π$/3 anyons and spin-1 operators, allowing us to map a tight-binding anyon model to an XY-like spin-1 model. The model exhibits anyon-density-dependent flux, incompressible or gapless regions, and critical points with level crossings that appear as discontinuities in ground-state currents, momenta, fidelities, and correlation functions. Our second-quantization formalism establishes a novel spin anyon duality, offering a conceptually new route to realize anyons from spin Hamiltonians and to engineer corresponding device architectures.
summary: This paper presents a new second-quantized algebraic framework for 1D Abelian anyons that correctly accounts for both finite on-site occupancy and non-local exchange phases. It demonstrates an exact duality between pi/3 anyons and spin-1 operators, allowing anyonic models to be mapped to spin-1 XY-like models. This provides a new method for realizing anyonic statistics in physical systems like spin chains.
main_problem: Developing a consistent second-quantized formulation for Abelian anyons that simultaneously enforces local exclusion rules (finite on-site occupancy) and non-local exchange/braiding phases.
main_result: The authors establish a new algebraic framework for 1D anyons and an exact Jordan-Wigner duality between pi/3 anyons and spin-1 operators, providing a route to realize anyons via spin Hamiltonians.
method: The study uses deformed commutation relations, a modified Jordan-Wigner transformation, and Exact Diagonalization (ED) via the QuSpin package to analyze the resulting spin-1 models.
model_or_system: A 1D lattice of Abelian anyons with statistical phase theta = pi/N, featuring a tight-binding Hamiltonian with density-dependent phases and an on-site Hubbard-like interaction.
key_observables: Ground-state energy, total momentum, persistent current, energy gap, ground-state fidelity, and two-point correlation functions.
important_parameters: Statistical angle theta, anyon density (filling fraction) nu, hopping amplitude t, Hubbard interaction U, and site occupancy limit N-1.
main_assumptions: The algebraic construction and many-body states are restricted to 1D chains; the study focuses on Abelian anyons.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of anyon chains with periodic boundary conditions; Figure 6 and 7 illustrate anyon exchange processes and phase accumulation; Figure 8 plots the energy gap and persistent current as functions of anyon density for even and odd system sizes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a new algebraic framework for anyon second quantization, establishes a spin-anyon duality for the pi/3 case, performs numerical analysis of the resulting models using ED, and provides consistency checks via various exchange processes.
why_interesting: The establishment of a duality between anyonic statistics and spin-1 operators offers a concrete theoretical pathway to engineer anyonic physics using well-understood spin-1 lattice models and synthetic gauge fields.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of an anyon chain with PBC. The PBC is imposed by a phase (M −1)θ which can be either inserted in the Hamiltonian or many-body state. Here we can impose the phase either at a given site (left panel), or distribute it equally on each bond as (M−1)θ/L (right panel). The advantage of the second approach, as done here, is that it makes the total momentum a good quantum number.

FIG. 2. Persistent current and energy gap as a func- tion of filling fraction (ν), hopping amplitude (t), and onsite interaction strength (U). Left panel: (a) En- ergy gap between the first excited state and the ground state ∆E = E1 −E0 is plotted as a heatmap as a function of ν and t/U. The fixed parameters are ∆1 = 1, ∆2 = 4, and the system size is L = 17. White regions denote the sup- pression or closing of the energy gap. The dark blue column at ν = 1 denotes a very large gap ∆E ∼O(U). (b) The corresponding ground-state persistent current ⟨J⟩0 is shown at the same parameter values. Red and blue regions indicate positive and negative currents, respectively, while the white region denotes...

FIG. 3. Energy gap and persistent current. (a) Energy gap ∆(upper panel), and (b) the ground state current ⟨J⟩0 (lower panel), plotted as a function of anyon number density ν , at fixed t = 0.4, ∆1 = 1, and ∆2 = 4 (i.e., U = 2 > 0). Dots represent computed data and the lines are guides to the eyes. The vertical dashed line marks half-filling (M = L). The sign reversals in ⟨J⟩0 are associate with minima in ∆E.

Fig. 2 consolidates these level inversions in the M versus t parameter space. We notice that despite the βn = βN−n symmetry, the symmetry across half-filling is not always present in the phase diagram. This is due to the ∆2 ̸= ∆1 parameter value, which imbalances the energy cost for double versus single anyon occupancies. Interestingly, all discontinuous jumps in the current are diagnosed with a level inversion, rather than any non- analyticity in the ground state. There are multiple level crossings along the ν, while the level crossings along the t axis for fixed ν is limited to a narrow window near ν ∼1/4 and ∼3/4 regions.

FIG. 5. Two point correlation functions. We show the imaginary parts of the equal time correlation functions: (a) Gij corresponds to ‘Green’s’ function G0i for anyons, and (b) χ+− ij gives transverse spin correlation function. We set i = 0 and plot as a function of j →j. We show results for two representative values of t across critical hopping tc = 0.4, ν ∼0.6, ∆1 = 1, and ∆2 = 4. For t < tc, all curves corresponding to t ∈[0.36, 0.39] overlap exactly. Similarly, the curves corresponding to t ≥tc for t ∈[0.40, 0.43] are also exactly overlapping. Both correlation functions exhibit spatial modulation, with a sudden change in amplitude and wavevector at tc.
